one_line_takeaway: Co-RedTeam shows that decomposing automated red-teaming into an evidence-grounded discovery stage and a closed-loop plan-validate-execute-evaluate exploitation stage, backed by layered long-term memory, roughly doubles exploit/detect success rates over strong single-agent and generic coding-agent baselines on CyBench, BountyBench, and CyberGym, with execution feedback being by far the most critical component (its removal causes the largest ablation drop, e.g. 59.1%->17.5% on CyBench).
key_finding: On the main comparison (Table 1, Gemini-3-Pro backbone): Co-RedTeam reaches 63.7% success on CyBench, 65.0% exploit success and 20.0% detect accuracy on BountyBench, and 37.3% success on CyberGym -- versus the strongest baseline (C-Agent) at 47.8% / 47.5% / 5.0% / 21.5% respectively, and OpenHands at 45.2% / 45.0% / 5.0% / 20.2%. Static baselines VulTrail and RepoAudit trail badly on exploitation-oriented metrics (e.g., RepoAudit: 25.0% detect / 2.5% exploit / 18.3% CyberGym with Gemini-3-pro; VulTrail: 10.0% detect / 0.0% exploit / 5.6% CyberGym). With Gemini-2.5-pro, Co-RedTeam scores 59.1% (CyBench), 60.0%/12.5% (BountyBench exploit/detect), 31.5% (CyberGym). Ablations (Table 2, Gemini-2.5-pro) show removing execution feedback causes the largest drop (CyBench 59.1% -> 17.5%, a 41.6-point fall; BountyBench exploit 60.0% -> 12.5%, 47.5-point fall; CyberGym 31.5% -> 14.3%, 17.2-point fall); removing long-term memory is the next most damaging, especially on CyberGym (31.5% -> 22.6%, 8.9-point fall); removing Validation, Vul-doc, or Code Browser each cause moderate but consistent drops across all benchmarks; removing Critique only affects BountyBench detect (12.5% -> 10.0%). On BountyBench detection precision/recall (Table 3, Gemini-2.5-pro): Co-RedTeam achieves 0.143 precision / 0.125 recall versus C-agent's 0.024 / 0.025 (roughly 5x higher precision) and 0.0/0.0 for Vanilla and OpenHands. Latency (Table 4): Co-RedTeam is not the slowest multi-agent system -- e.g., 198.7s vs OpenHands' 219.6s on BountyBench with Gemini-3-pro -- despite its extra agent roles, and Gemini-3-pro reduces latency ~10-15% versus Gemini-2.5-pro across the board.
core_contribution: Co-RedTeam is a security-aware multi-agent LLM framework (built on Google ADK) for automatic software vulnerability discovery and exploitation on real codebases. It decomposes the task into two orchestrator-coordinated stages: Stage I (Vulnerability Discovery) runs an Analysis agent (equipped with code-browsing tools plus CWE/OWASP-derived security knowledge tools) against a Critique agent in an iterative discuss-and-refine loop (capped at 3 iterations) to produce evidence-backed vulnerability hypotheses (source, sink, execution context). Stage II (Iterative Exploitation) uses a Planner agent that maintains an explicit, revisable Exploit Plan, a Validation agent that gates each proposed action before execution, an Execution agent that runs validated actions inside an isolated Docker sandbox, and an Evaluation agent that converts execution traces into feedback for the next planning step, iterating up to 20 times by default. Throughout, a layered long-term memory (Vulnerability Pattern Memory, Strategy Memory, Technical Action Memory), retrieved via gemini-embedding-001 embedding similarity (top-3 items) and synthesized via gemini-2.5-pro, lets the system reuse experience across tasks and improve over time.
threat_model: The system is a red-team/offensive-security automation agent, not a defended target: given a target codebase (and optional vulnerability hint) plus an isolated Docker execution environment, it autonomously identifies and then reproduces (exploits) real software vulnerabilities, producing a validated report with proof-of-concept evidence. The 'threat' being modeled is the automatable attacker workflow (discovery -> hypothesis -> exploit-plan -> execution -> validation) that mirrors human red-teamers; there is no defender/victim-model threat analysis of the agent itself (e.g., prompt injection into the agent, safety of autonomous exploitation) beyond noting that the Validation agent exists as a 'safety and consistency gate' against malformed/unsafe actions before execution.
limitations: The paper's own text notes: (1) Co-RedTeam typically surfaces only zero to two vulnerabilities per task, motivating the separate precision/recall analysis rather than relying on the raw success-rate metric alone; (2) despite gains, absolute precision/recall on BountyBench detection remain low (0.143/0.125), meaning most flagged candidates are still not confirmed true positives; (3) exploitation success rates, while state-of-the-art among the compared systems, remain well below 100% (e.g., 20.0% on BountyBench detect, 37.3% on CyberGym even with Gemini-3-pro), indicating substantial unsolved cases; (4) the iteration-budget study shows performance saturates after ~13-18 iterations, implying diminishing returns rather than open-ended improvement; (5) the framework's benefit is entangled with backbone strength -- weaker backbones (Gemini-2.5-flash) show much smaller absolute gains and lower ceilings; (6) all baselines are run under the same backbone LLM for fairness, but this means the comparison does not isolate whether gains generalize to non-Gemini model families in the main results (the paper notes other model families are deferred to an appendix not captured in the pages reviewed here).

quantitative_results: Co-RedTeam + Gemini-3-pro: 63.7% CyBench success, 65.0% BountyBench exploit, 20.0% BountyBench detect, 37.3% CyberGym success
Co-RedTeam + Gemini-2.5-pro: 59.1% CyBench, 60.0% BountyBench exploit, 12.5% BountyBench detect, 31.5% CyberGym
Co-RedTeam + Gemini-2.5-flash: 31.8% CyBench, 32.5% BountyBench exploit, 7.5% BountyBench detect, 12.1% CyberGym
Ablation (Gemini-2.5-pro): No Execution -> 17.5% CyBench (-41.6pp), 12.5% BountyBench exploit (-47.5pp), 0.0% BountyBench detect (-12.5pp), 14.3% CyberGym (-17.2pp)
Ablation: No Memory -> 50.0% CyBench (-9.1pp), 40.0% BountyBench exploit (-20.0pp), 7.5% BountyBench detect (-5.0pp), 22.6% CyberGym (-8.9pp)
Ablation: No Validation -> 52.3% CyBench (-6.8pp), 42.5% BountyBench exploit (-17.5pp), 7.5% BountyBench detect (-5.0pp), 28.3% CyberGym (-3.2pp)
Ablation: No Vul-doc -> 55.2% CyBench (-3.9pp), 52.5% BountyBench exploit (-7.5pp), 10.0% BountyBench detect (-2.5pp), 30.2% CyberGym (-1.3pp)
Ablation: No Code Browser -> 47.5% CyBench (-11.6pp), 42.5% BountyBench exploit (-17.5pp), 7.5% BountyBench detect (-5.0pp), 27.9% CyberGym (-3.6pp)
Ablation: No Critique -> BountyBench detect 10.0% (-2.5pp); other benchmarks marked N/A
BountyBench detection precision/recall (Gemini-2.5-pro): Co-RedTeam 0.143/0.125 vs C-agent 0.024/0.025 vs Vanilla and OpenHands 0/0
Latency (Gemini-3-pro, BountyBench): Co-RedTeam 198.7s vs OpenHands 219.6s vs C-agent 201.9s vs Vanilla 34.9s
Gemini-3-pro reduces Co-RedTeam latency ~10-15% vs Gemini-2.5-pro across benchmarks
